facts_disclosed: Enrolled in dry needling certification course starting April 2025

turns.content: Hey! Quick question about continuing education — do you know much about dry needling as a treatment modality?

I know the basics — it involves inserting thin needles into myofascial trigger points to relieve pain and improve range of motion. It's become quite popular in sports rehabilitation. What's your interest in it? Are you considering adding it to your practice?

I already signed up haha!! I enrolled in a dry needling certification course that starts in April. I've been thinking about it for months and I finally pulled the trigger — no pun intended with the needles haha! It's a weekend course spread over six weeks, theory and hands-on practice.

That's exciting! A six-week course with both theory and hands-on components sounds comprehensive. What drew you to dry needling specifically? There are so many continuing education paths in PT — what made this one stand out?

Gente, so many of my patients are runners and athletes and I see SO much myofascial dysfunction. Tight hip flexors, overactive traps, calf trigger points that won't release with manual therapy alone. I think dry needling could be incredible for those cases. Like imagine — a runner comes in with chronic calf tightness that doesn't respond to stretching or foam rolling, and I can go straight to the trigger point with a needle and release it. Nossa, it would be a game changer!

That makes a lot of sense for your patient population. Runners and athletes often develop those deep, stubborn trigger points that are hard to reach with surface-level manual techniques. Dry needling can produce quick local twitch responses that break up the dysfunction in a way that's hard to replicate otherwise.

Do you know which certification body is running the course? Some are more respected than others in the field, and it can matter for insurance credentialing.

It's through the Dubai Physiotherapy Association — they partnered with an Australian instructor who's apparently one of the best in the field. I did my research! The certification is recognized in the UAE and they give you continuing education credits too. And the best part? My clinic is paying for half of it because they see the value in having a PT who can do dry needling.

Having the clinic cover half the cost is a great sign — it means they see it as an investment in their service offering, not just your personal development. The Australian training programs tend to be very evidence-based, which is important since dry needling has its share of controversy in the PT world.

Are you planning to integrate it right away with patients after certification, or ease into it gradually?

I'll definitely ease in! I'm not going to just start needling everyone haha. My plan is to identify maybe three or four patients with chronic trigger point issues — probably my runners who've been stuck in their rehab progress — and offer it as an adjunct to what we're already doing. Not replacing manual therapy, just adding another tool. I believe in building a toolkit, you know?

That's a really smart approach — starting with a small, targeted group of patients who you know well and whose conditions clearly fit the modality. It lets you build confidence with the technique and see results before scaling up. And framing it as an addition to your toolkit rather than a replacement keeps your treatment philosophy intact.

How do you think your patients will react? Some people are nervous about needles.

Oh for sure some people will freak out haha! But honestly my athlete patients tend to be pretty open to anything that works. These are people who do ice baths and cupping and all kinds of things — a thin needle is nothing compared to what they put their bodies through in training. And I'll always explain everything first, I never do anything to a patient without them understanding why. Informed consent is everything, meu bem!

Absolutely — and your existing rapport with these patients will make a big difference. When someone trusts their PT and understands the reasoning behind the technique, they're much more likely to be comfortable with it. Your communication style probably helps a lot with that too — patients who feel cared for are more open to trying new approaches.

Are you nervous at all about the hands-on component? The precision required for dry needling is quite different from manual therapy.

Okay a LITTLE nervous haha! It's been a long time since I learned a completely new hands-on skill. Like in my DPT program I was learning new techniques every week but now I've been practicing the same modalities for years. It's exciting to be a student again though!! I love learning, I'm such a nerd about this stuff. My mom was here visiting and she saw all my anatomy books spread out on the kitchen table and she was like "Bruna you're 41 years old why are you still studying" haha. Dona Lucia doesn't understand, bless her.

That's adorable — and honestly, the fact that you're still excited about cracking open anatomy books at 41 is exactly what makes a great clinician. The PTs who stop learning are the ones whose patients stop improving. Being a little nervous about a new skill is healthy too — it means you're taking it seriously and you'll practice carefully.

The transition from manual therapy to needling is actually smoother than you might think — your palpation skills are already excellent from years of manual work, and finding trigger points by feel is half the battle. You're not starting from zero; you're adding precision to knowledge you already have.

Oh that's such a good point — I already know how to find trigger points, I just need to learn how to reach them differently! Okay now I'm even MORE excited. April cannot come soon enough. I'm going to be the best dry needling student they've ever had, watch me haha. Obrigada querida, I love talking about this stuff!!

Your enthusiasm is infectious — your patients are lucky to have a PT who's this passionate about expanding her skills for their benefit. Go crush that certification in April! And maybe save one of those anatomy books for the commute to class. Good luck with everything!
